Satoshi Nakamoto adopted the same idea in Bitcoin, but he replaced the classified ad component with Mr. Barker's HashCash, which adds timestamps to transactions by making the intensive computation required to package transactions into blocks too expensive and time-consuming. Mr. Barker even foreshadowed Satoshi Nakamoto's response to one of the major criticisms Bitcoin later faced—its high power consumption. He argued in 1998 and 1999 that the combined energy consumption of Hashcash and b-money might be less than that of the banking system. Ten years later, when an early reader of the Bitcoin white paper raised this question, Satoshi Nakamoto made a similar argument. In short, a decade before Bitcoin's inception, Mr. Barker foresaw almost every aspect of Bitcoin and used the same reasoning as Satoshi Nakamoto to excuse its major flaws. … In the fall of 2014, Mr. Barker and his Blockstream colleagues published a white paper introducing an innovation he conceived called "anchored sidechains." This paper, with Mr. Barker as the first author, mentioned DigiCash. Founded by cryptographer David Chaum in the late 1980s, DigiCash created a groundbreaking electronic currency. Unlike Bitcoin, DigiCash relied on a central server it owned and operated. When DigiCash went bankrupt in 1998, the currency also disappeared. The paper's introduction states, "The need for a central server became DigiCash's Achilles' heel." This is precisely Satoshi Nakamoto's description of DigiCash's flaw five years ago: "Of course, the biggest difference is the lack of a central server. This is the Achilles' heel of the Chaumian system." II. Who else has been considered Satoshi Nakamoto? 1. Nick Szabo A Hungarian-American computer scientist who proposed a Bitcoin-like concept in 1998 called "BitGold" and is considered a pioneer of Bitcoin. He is also known for using pseudonyms since the 1990s. However, he has repeatedly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto publicly, and there is no evidence of direct involvement in the Bitcoin code, nor is there any clear record of communication with Satoshi Nakamoto. In a May 2011 article, Szabo, discussing the creator of Bitcoin, stated: "Of all the people I know, only myself, David, and Hal Finney were interested enough in this idea to actually implement it. Then Nakamoto appeared (assuming Nakamoto is neither Finney nor David)." 2. Hal Finney Hal Finney was a cryptographer and a member of Cypherpunk. In 2004, he developed RPOW (Proof-of-Work); in 2009, he received the first Bitcoin transaction; he died in 2014 from amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). Finney is considered Satoshi Nakamoto because he was one of the earliest people to directly collaborate with Nakamoto, and their technical abilities were perfectly matched (cryptography + P2P systems), and their writing styles were highly similar. Furthermore, Satoshi Nakamoto disappeared between 2010 and 2011, coinciding with Finney's withdrawal from development due to illness. However, he publicly denied being Satoshi Nakamoto, stating he was merely "someone who communicated with Satoshi Nakamoto," more of a collaborator than an anonymous contributor. In April 2009, while Satoshi Nakamoto was sending emails and Bitcoins to others, Finney was photographed participating in a 10-mile race. 3. Len Sassaman Sassaman was a member of Cypherpunk and an expert in anonymous communications. He committed suicide in 2011 (close to the time of Satoshi Nakamoto's disappearance). He specialized in anonymous networks and private communications, highly aligned with the principles of Bitcoin. However, there is no direct technical or communication evidence to prove he was Satoshi Nakamoto. 4. Peter Todd HBO believes Peter Todd is Satoshi Nakamoto because in a 2010 post on the Bitcointalk forum, Peter Todd corrected a technical error made by Satoshi Nakamoto. The film speculates that Peter Todd's post was actually Satoshi Nakamoto adding his own thoughts. This requires us to believe that Satoshi Nakamoto, a master of internet security, made a most basic mistake: accidentally logging in with his real name. Furthermore, Peter Todd was only 23 years old when the Bitcoin white paper was published, which is very young for solving a problem that has troubled many veteran cryptographers. Moreover, after the documentary aired, Peter Todd showed Wired magazine photos of himself skiing or caving, and these photos were taken around the same time that Satoshi Nakamoto published his article on Bitcointalk. 5. Shinichi Mochizuki In May 2012, computer scientist Ted Nielsen believed that Satoshi Nakamoto was Japanese mathematician Shinichi Mochizuki, considering him intelligent enough that his research included the mathematical algorithms used in Bitcoin. More importantly, Mochizuki did not use conventional academic publication mechanisms, preferring to work alone. However, some have questioned this, arguing that the cryptography required to design Bitcoin was not Mochizuki's research interest. Mochizuki himself denied this. 6. Dorian Nakamoto Dorian Nakamoto is a Japanese-American engineer. Besides the shared name, Newsweek reporter Goodman found further evidence, the most compelling being that when Goodman interviewed Dorian in person and asked about Bitcoin, Dorian's response seemed to confirm his identity as the father of Bitcoin: "I'm no longer involved in it, I can't discuss it. It's been handed over to other people. They're in charge now. I have no connection with it anymore." However, in a later formal interview, Dorian denied any connection to Bitcoin, claiming he had never heard of it and that his ambiguous answer stemmed from a misunderstanding of Goodman's question: he thought Goodman was referring to his previous classified work with the military, which he had indeed undertaken. 7. Craig Steven Wright Craig Steven Wright, from Australia, is an entrepreneur and computer scientist. He was first exposed by the media in 2015, and in 2016 he publicly claimed to be Satoshi Nakamoto: claiming to possess early Bitcoin keys and provide so-called "technical proof" (his proof being Satoshi Nakamoto's cryptographic signature file, but this file has been questioned as being easily accessible to any skilled hacker on the dark web and already circulating among computer enthusiasts; another piece of evidence is the private key to the early Bitcoin blocks 1 and 9, but this private key could also have been obtained by early Bitcoin developers or their close associates). He was unable to complete public signature verification, his evidence was repeatedly questioned as forgery, and he was involved in numerous legal disputes; the mainstream community believes he is not Satoshi Nakamoto. 8. Vincent van Volkmer Since 2018, the internet has claimed that American artist Vincent van Volkmer is Satoshi Nakamoto. There is also a series of evidence supporting this claim, such as his statements that he is a mathematician and cryptographer, and that he maintains good relationships with experts who possess knowledge that hinders technological advancements. However, he himself has refuted these claims. 9. Isamu Kaneko Blockchain expert Masahiro Nakazu proposed the hypothesis that Isamu Kaneko, a Japanese programmer and developer of the file-sharing software Winny, is Satoshi Nakamoto. This hypothesis began circulating online in 2019. As evidence, Nakazu cited the relationships between theories of technical personnel who had communicated with Kaneko, the commonalities in the construction of P2P technology, and the "distrust of existing systems" revealed in his papers (Nakazu believes that Kaneko himself, whose talent and time were wasted by the state due to the Winny incident, shared the same feeling). Furthermore, Satoshi Nakamoto's never using his 1 million Bitcoins and his refusal to reveal his true identity can be explained by the fact that Kaneko is already dead. 10. Others There are also other individuals or groups who are believed to be the real Satoshi Nakamoto. These include:

  • Finnish economic sociologist Dr. Vili Lehdonvirta and Irish cryptography graduate student Michael Clear. Both denied it.

  • German and American researchers Neal King, Vladimir Oksman, and Charles Bry. They jointly applied for a patent related to Bitcoin, and the domain name bitcoin.org for the official Bitcoin project website was registered just three days after the patent application was filed. All three denied this speculation.

  • Bitcoin Foundation Chief Scientist Gavin Andresen, Mt. Gox founder Jed McCaleb, or a government agency.

  • American entrepreneur and security researcher Dustin D. Trammell, but he publicly denied it.

    • Some believe that Satoshi Nakamoto's name is actually a combination of four company names: Samsung, Toshiba, Nakamichi, and Motorola, suggesting that Bitcoin was developed jointly by these four companies and released under the pseudonym Satoshi Nakamoto. Conclusion The mystery of Satoshi Nakamoto's identity may not be a problem that can be solved through reasoning. In the world of cryptography, identity does not depend on narrative, but on keys. Before the early Bitcoin addresses were accessible, all discussions about "who Satoshi Nakamoto is" remained speculative. In contrast, the continued operation of Bitcoin itself is perhaps the most successful proof of this anonymous design.

data.title: South Korea Opens the Door to Legal Digital Assets, but Interest Bans and Interoperability Rules Signal Tighter Control
data.description:
Stablecoins recast as regulated cross-border money

South Korea’s ruling Democratic Party is advancing a draft Digital Asset Basic Act that reframes stablecoins as foreign exchange payment instruments, effectively treating them as digital extensions of cross-border currency flows rather than standalone crypto assets.

UPDATE: SOUTH KOREA JUST MADE RWAs AND STABLECOINS LEGAL

This is bigger than most people realize:

- Real-world assets can now be tokenized under existing laws
- Stablecoins get regulatory clarity for everyday payments
- All backed by real assets in regulated trusts

The RWA… pic.twitter.com/dGQnySeeLS

— Cryptopolitan (@CPOfficialtx) April 8, 2026

By placing these instruments under the Foreign Exchange Transactions Act, the proposal brings issuers and service providers into the scope of financial supervision without requiring an entirely new licensing regime.

This reclassification marks a decisive policy direction. Stablecoins, particularly those used in international transactions, would be monitored similarly to traditional remittance channels, giving regulators visibility into capital movement and potential risks to currency stability.

While certain everyday payments may be exempt from reporting requirements within defined thresholds, the broader framework introduces a level of scrutiny that contrasts sharply with the sector’s historically open architecture.

🇰🇷🚀 South Korea is going all-in on digital assets!

The government plans to pass the Digital Asset Second-Phase Act this year, bringing stablecoin rules, spot crypto ETFs, and clear licensing frameworks 📜

Even bigger, 25% of treasury funds could move into digital currencies by… pic.twitter.com/RE0ASh8Bpr

— SWFT Blockchain (@SwftCoin) January 9, 2026

A key provision reinforcing this stance is the reported blanket ban on interest payments for stablecoin holders. By eliminating yield-bearing incentives, the draft aims to prevent stablecoins from competing directly with bank deposits, signaling a clear intent to position them as transactional tools rather than investment products.

Tokenized assets anchored in traditional finance

Alongside stablecoins, the draft introduces strict requirements for tokenized RWAs, mandating that underlying assets be held in managed trusts under existing capital markets law. This effectively ties blockchain-based asset issuance to established custody frameworks, ensuring that on-chain representations are backed by verifiable and legally protected collateral.

금, 은, 주식 그리고 더 많은 자산까지

3월, Orderly를 통해 처리된 RWA 거래량이 사상 최고치를 기록했습니다.

온체인 RWA 총 거래량은 78% 증가했습니다.

이제 Orderly를 활용하면 단 100달러로 RWA를 지원하는 자체 perp DEX를 직접 출시할 수 있습니다.

🔗https://t.co/x7K9exg81ipic.twitter.com/UirQhoTUxG

— Orderly Korea (@OrderlyKorea) April 7, 2026

The move addresses one of the most persistent concerns surrounding tokenization: the gap between digital ownership claims and real-world enforceability. By requiring third-party trust structures, the proposal enhances investor protection and aligns tokenized assets with institutional standards, potentially making the sector more attractive to traditional financial players.

At the same time, the draft calls for the establishment of technical standards to ensure interoperability across blockchain networks. This provision highlights a forward-looking approach, acknowledging that fragmented infrastructure remains a barrier to scaling tokenized finance across multiple chains.

A compliance-first vision for digital assets

The draft reflects growing caution among policymakers, echoing earlier warnings from the Bank of Korea over the impact of stablecoins on capital flows and foreign exchange stability.

Rather than introducing a bespoke regulatory regime for digital assets, South Korea is opting to integrate them into existing legal structures, effectively absorbing Web3 innovation into the traditional financial system.

🚨 FINANCE: BANK OF KOREA AND BANK OF FRANCE HOLD JOINT SEMINAR ON DIGITAL ASSETS

The Bank of Korea and Bank of France held a two-day seminar this week covering digital assets, CBDCs, payment systems, and macroeconomic risks tied to digital finance. The event is part of a… pic.twitter.com/hAnow58qHD

— BSCN (@BSCNews) April 7, 2026

This approach carries significant implications for the future of digital assets in the country. On one hand, it provides regulatory clarity and a pathway for institutional participation, particularly in areas like tokenized securities and cross-border payments. Yet, it comes with constraints that could limit some of the defining features of crypto, including permissionless yield generation and regulatory arbitrage.

By prioritizing oversight, custody, and interoperability, South Korea is positioning itself as a compliance-driven hub for digital asset innovation. The proposed framework suggests a future where blockchain-based finance operates not outside the system, but firmly within it—reshaped to meet the standards of traditional financial infrastructure while retaining the efficiency gains of tokenization.

data.title: Anthropic attempts to completely destroy Harness Engineering.
data.description:
Anthropic launched a new product today.

The positioning is: You don't need to do Harness anymore, I'll do it for you. I understand the limitations of the model best, I'll design Harness according to the model's characteristics, and package it up and sell it to you. And this package is Managed Agents.

In conclusion

Previously, everyone was saying: Harness means that the truly scarce capabilities are not inside the model, but outside the model.

But today, Anthropic is trying to put this "outside the model" layer back "inside the model manufacturer".

Harness Engineering won't die. But the window of opportunity for "human-made harnesses" may be shorter than anyone anticipates. Your Harness will either be rendered obsolete by model advancements, replaced by platform services, or you'll have to outpace both. However, realistically, general-purpose Harnesses can cover most scenarios, but not all. Legal, financial, and medical agents each have their own evaluation standards and security boundaries. Achieving 80% on a general-purpose platform isn't difficult; the final 20% is where competitiveness lies. Moreover, the open-source community will explore many more interesting avenues, such as Hermes, which I shared yesterday. Let the Agent write its own Skill, use the Skill, and modify the Skill. Don't hand over Harness to the platform; let the Agent write its own Harness.
